title: Restaurant High Chair
summary: A compact wooden high chair in the classic restaurant style, built from 1x2s with 5° splayed legs. Light enough to move, stable enough to trust.
description:
This copies the wooden high chair every restaurant used to keep by the door — small, stackable-looking, and stable. It's built almost entirely from 1x2s, with a 1x10 (or three glued 1x4s) for the seat and a 1x4 seat back. The stability comes from a gentle 5° splay on the legs, which widens the base at the floor.

The cut list is precise about the 5° cuts, and reading it carefully is the whole job: the legs are cut 5° at both ends parallel to each other, while the side rails are cut 5° at both ends NOT parallel (measured long-point to long-point), and a couple of pieces are beveled rather than mitered. Set your saw to 5° and cut each category of piece together so they match; mixing up parallel and non-parallel ends is the way to get a chair that rocks.

All the joinery is pocket holes with 1-1/4" screws, two per joint end, glued and clamped. Build the two side assemblies first and make them identical. Expect to fill the pocket holes — the source needed three applications of filler to get them flush before paint. And because it's a high chair, it is not finished until it has a sturdy center strap and a safety belt; the wood holds the child up, the restraint keeps them safe.
timeLabel: 4–6 hrs plus filling, sanding, and paint

tools.note: Every joint. 1-1/4" screws, two per end.
For the 5° angle and bevel cuts; a miter saw is easier if you have one.

materials.name: Wood filler
materials.note: Expect three applications to fill the pocket holes flush.

materials.name: Safety strap and belt
materials.note: Required — a high chair needs a center strap and a restraint.

cutList.note: Both ends 5° parallel to each other.
5° ends NOT parallel, long point to long point.
5° ends NOT parallel, long point to long point.
5° ends NOT parallel, long point to long point.
5° ends NOT parallel, long point to long point.
5° ends NOT parallel, long point to long point.
Ends beveled 5°, NOT parallel, short point to short point.
Ends beveled 5°, NOT parallel, short point to short point.

steps.title: Build the two sides
steps.body: Cut all the 5° pieces, keeping straight which ends are parallel and which aren't. Drill two pocket holes in each end of the horizontal rails. Mark the leg positions, clamp, glue, and screw with 1-1/4" pocket screws. Build both sides and make them identical.

steps.title: Add the bottom supports
steps.body: Drill two pocket holes in each end of the bottom supports. Mark the sides 3-1/2" up, then glue, clamp, and screw the supports to the sides, centering them on the width. This is the trickier joint — take your time squaring it.

steps.title: Attach the seat and back
steps.body: Set the beveled seat in place and fasten it, then drill pocket holes in the seat back and attach it with glue and 1-1/4" pocket screws. Check the back angle feels right.

steps.title: Add the front rail
steps.body: Drill pocket holes on both ends of the front piece and attach it to the tops of the front legs with glue and 1-1/4" pocket screws. This closes the seat box and stiffens the frame.

steps.title: Fill, finish, and add restraints
steps.body: Fill the pocket holes — plan on three applications to get them flush — then sand thoroughly with 120 grit. Prime, paint, and add a sprayed topcoat. Finally, fit a sturdy center strap and a safety belt. It is not a usable high chair until it can restrain the child.

images.alt: Wooden restaurant-style high chair with splayed legs
